text.title: Stakeholder Analysis: Git-Native Register and Agent Patterns
text.summary: Git-native register pattern, recommended subagents, discovery and engagement-plan prompt patterns, and AI gotchas.

rule.statement: Store the stakeholder register as YAML in git (stakeholders/register.yaml); subagents emit patches, never overwrite the register directly. Each transition (added, attitude shift, escalated) is committed with a one-line rationale.
rule.rationale: git log -- stakeholders/ becomes the relationship history; git blame shows who reclassified whom; CODEOWNERS gates sponsor sign-off on attitude changes. A wiki or spreadsheet cannot provide any of these audit capabilities.

rule.statement: Refresh the register quarterly at minimum; set a calendar trigger that opens an issue assigned to the BA. In M&A and reorg contexts, refresh every 2-4 weeks.
rule.rationale: Stakeholder registers drift in 2-6 weeks in fast-moving organizations; a static register goes stale and becomes wrong, not just old. Automated refresh triggers prevent the register from becoming a liability.

rule.statement: Never publish names + attitudes outside the BA and sponsor; treat the register as confidential. PII regulations (GDPR Art. 9) may apply if attitude implies political opinion or health status.
rule.rationale: Attitude records about named individuals are sensitive personal data; public exposure damages relationships and may create legal liability in GDPR-jurisdiction projects.

example.note: Discovery prompt constrains to real org chart entries, flags unknown attitudes, and surfaces hidden stakeholders as suggestions rather than entries.
Engagement-plan prompt uses quadrant mapping as default override policy.
example.code: Given the register below, produce the engagement table. Quadrant mapping: HH=Manage Closely (weekly 1:1), HL=Keep Satisfied (biweekly summary), LH=Keep Informed (monthly digest), LL=Monitor (release notes only). Override only with explicit rationale.

antipattern.description: Agent invents plausible-sounding stakeholders ("VP of Product Excellence") not present in the org chart.
antipattern.reason: Hallucinated stakeholders produce engagement plans with no real recipients; always seed with a real org chart export and reject any name not in the source list unless flagged suggested_to_verify.

antipattern.description: Agent over-classifies all stakeholders as "supportive" (politeness bias).
antipattern.reason: LLMs are trained on polite discourse and default to positive sentiment; use a 4-way enum (+/0/-/unknown) with unknown as the default and require evidence for + or -.

antipattern.description: Agent conflates stakeholder with persona ("As a Busy Manager persona...").
antipattern.reason: A stakeholder is a real named human or role with decision power; a persona is a synthesized archetype for design. Conflating them produces engagement plans targeting archetypes, not real people with authority.

antipattern.description: Agent equates influence with seniority — ranks CFO highest influence by default.
antipattern.reason: Influence flows through indirect networks; the CFO's EA may have more influence on calendar decisions than the CFO. Force the agent to consider gatekeepers, information brokers, and informal leaders, not just org chart level.

antipattern.description: Agent bulk-reclassifies 50 stakeholders in a single run.
antipattern.reason: Bulk reclassification of attitude fields is reputationally toxic if wrong; cap agent actions to 5 stakeholders per run and require human review on the diff.

rule.statement: Require human approval at three points: adding a new stakeholder, changing attitude from unknown to positive or negative, and escalating a conflict to sponsor. Agent prepares the patch; BA or sponsor approves the merge.
rule.rationale: Stakeholder relationship decisions have political consequences; automated execution without human sign-off can damage trust, violate confidentiality expectations, and create legal liability.
